title: HashMaps
description: HashMap<K,V> is the most-used data structure in interviews for O(1) lookups. Master insert, get, contains_key, the entry API for elegant upserts, and iteration patterns to solve frequency counting, two-sum, and graph problems.

sections.title: Creating and inserting
sections.explanation: Create with `HashMap::new()`. Use `.insert(key, value)` to add entries — it returns the old value as Option<V> if the key existed. Access with `.get(&key)` which returns `Option<&V>`, or use `map[&key]` (panics if missing). Always prefer `.get()` in interview code for safety.

exercise.prompt: Create a HashMap<&str, i32> mapping "apple"->3, "banana"->5, "cherry"->2. Print the value for "banana" using .get(), check if "date" exists, and print the map length.

validation.message: get("banana") returns Some(5), "date" doesn't exist, length is 3
Use HashMap::new(), insert(), get(), and contains_key()

sections.title: Entry API for frequency counting
sections.explanation: The **entry API** is Rust's elegant way to handle "insert if missing, update if present". Use `map.entry(key).or_insert(default)` to get a mutable reference, then dereference with `*` to modify. This is the go-to pattern for frequency counting — the most common HashMap interview pattern.

exercise.prompt: Count the frequency of each character in the string "abracadabra". Print the count for 'a' and 'b'.

validation.message: Use the entry API with or_insert(0) and increment with += 1

exercise.hint: Initialize with or_insert(0) so the first occurrence starts at 0 then increments to 1.

sections.title: Iterating over a HashMap
sections.explanation: Iterate with `for (key, value) in &map` or use `.iter()`. For mutable iteration, use `for (key, value) in &mut map` (value is &mut V). You can also iterate just keys with `.keys()` or values with `.values()`. Note: HashMap iteration order is not guaranteed.

exercise.prompt: Create a HashMap with "x"->10, "y"->20, "z"->30. Use .values() to compute the sum of all values. Use .keys() to collect all keys into a sorted Vec. Print the sum and the sorted keys.

validation.message: Sum of values is 60, sorted keys are ["x", "y", "z"]
Use .values().sum() for the total and .keys().collect() with sort for ordered keys

exercise.hint: The code is complete. .values().sum() adds all values, .keys().collect() gathers keys.

sections.title: Two-sum with HashMap
sections.explanation: The classic two-sum pattern: for each number, check if `target - num` exists in the map. If yes, return the pair. If not, store `num -> index`. This O(n) approach is the gold standard for this problem and demonstrates effective HashMap usage to interviewers.

exercise.prompt: Complete the two_sum function. Test with nums = [3, 2, 4] and target = 6. Print the result tuple.

validation.message: nums[1]=2 + nums[2]=4 = 6, so the answer is (1, 2)
Check for complement in the map, then insert the current num with its index

exercise.hint: Insert the current number and its index: seen.insert(num, i).
